title: Food Labelling
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Food labelling provides consumers (0) with information about the products they purchase, including ingredients, nutritional content, and allergen warnings. Understanding these labels is essential for making informed dietary choices.||In most countries, food manufacturers are required (9) by law to list ingredients in descending order by weight. This means the first ingredient listed is present (10) in the greatest quantity. However, manufacturers sometimes use multiple types of sugar with different names to prevent 'sugar' from appearing as the first ingredient, (11) which can mislead consumers trying to reduce their sugar intake.||Nutritional labels typically show the amount of calories, fat, protein, and carbohydrates per serving. The problem is (12) that serving sizes on labels don't always match what people actually eat. A package that appears small might (13) be labelled as containing two or more servings, meaning consumers could accidentally consume far more calories than they realise.||Terms like 'natural', 'fresh', and 'healthy' are often used (14) on packaging, but these words may not be regulated as strictly as consumers assume. 'Organic' certification has clearer standards, although (15) what qualifies as organic varies between countries. 'Free-range' labels on eggs and meat can also be misleading, as the regulations governing these terms differ widely.||Consumer advocacy groups are pushing (16) for clearer, more standardised labelling systems that would make it easier for shoppers to make healthy choices quickly.

example.text: Food labelling provides consumers (0) information about the products they purchase.

questions.explanation: Passive voice: 'are required by law' indicates a legal obligation.
Expression 'present in quantity' indicates presence in a certain amount.
Relative pronoun referring to the practice of using multiple names.
Conjunction 'that' introduces a noun clause after 'is'.
Voz pasiva modal: 'might be labelled' expresa posibilidad.
Expression 'used on packaging' indicates the location of the terms.
Relative pronoun 'what' introduces a noun clause about qualification.
Expression 'pushing for' means to advocate or push for something.
